topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of damp earth and old bark hit Elias like a physical blow. He’d been walking along the edge of the woods, a familiar path winding toward the old oak, the one he’d painstakingly nurtured into a young sapling when he was ten. Now, where the vibrant green spire should have stood, there was only a jagged scar in the undergrowth, a raw wound in the landscape. Elias stopped dead, his breath catching in his throat. His hands, which had been clutching a smooth, grey stone, began to tremble so violently he had to press them against his chest to steady them. A coldness seeped into his bones, despite the mild afternoon sun. He took a hesitant step back, his eyes darting wildly into the dense shadows where the tree had been. "No," he whispered, the sound thin and reedy in the silence. "No, it can't be."

[story 2]

Sarah knelt at the base of the clearing, her fingers tracing the disturbed soil. The imprint of the trunk was gone, replaced by tangled roots and freshly churned dirt. A sickening lurch tightened in her stomach, stealing the air from her lungs. She stood up abruptly, stumbling slightly, and looked around the perimeter of the woods, searching for any sign of disturbance, any tell-tale sign of recent activity. Her heart hammered against her ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm. She forced herself to look back at the empty space. A strange, hollow ache settled deep within her chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. "This isn't right," she managed, her voice barely above a strained murmur. She backed away slowly, her gaze fixed on the empty space, unable to reconcile what she was seeing with what she remembered.

[story 3]

Mark approached the spot slowly, his pace measured, almost reverent, until he reached the center of the clearing. The sheer emptiness where the young tree had stood was overwhelming. He crouched down, brushing aside fallen leaves, his movements stiff and jerky. When he saw the evidence—the stark, unnatural break in the foliage—a wave of intense unease washed over him, making his skin prickle. He felt a sudden, sharp pressure behind his eyes. He stood up quickly, his boots crunching on dry debris, and turned to flee. Every sound in the surrounding woods seemed amplified, pressing in on him, and he could only focus on putting distance between himself and that desolate patch of ground.
